coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ciprofloxacin using the terbium-based coordination polymers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: An, Xinan
Zhu, Xinyue
Liu, Jiaojiao
Zou, Lina
Li, Gaiping
Ye, Baoxian
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile self-assembly through adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and luminol with Tb3+ was employed to construct a dual-ligand coordinated AMP-Tb-luminol coordination polymers (CPs), which emitted the typical fluorescence of luminol. Based on the sensitization effect of ciprofloxacin (CIP) on the luminescence of Tb3+, a ratiometric sensor was fabricated using the fluorescence of luminol as an inert reference. The fluorescent intensity ratios of Tb3+ to that of luminol enhanced linearly with the CIP concentration in the range from 5 nM to 2.5 μM with a lower limit of detection of 2 nM. In addition, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibited high selectivity for CIP, which could also be used to detect CIP in human blood serum (HBS) with satisfactory results. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of using dual-ligand coordination lanthanide (Ln)-based CPs for ratio-metric CIP assay, and this straightforward strategy may open up a new platform for designing the ratio-metric sensors based on the Ln CPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Ciprofloxacin
Lanthanide coordination polymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of CIP in aqueous solution and human blood serum (HBS)
Synthesis and characterization of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs
The response of AMP-Tb-luminol towards CIP and the possible mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21575130 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) AMP-Tb CPs, (B) AMP-Tb-luminol CPs.
Fluorescence spectra (λex = 345 nm) of AMP-Tb CPs (line a), AMP-Tb-luminol CPs (line b) and AMP-Tb-luminol CPs + CIP (line c) in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4).
FT-IR spectra of luminol (line a), AMP-Tb-luminol CPs (line b) and AMP (line c).
(A) FT-IR spectra of CIP (line a), AMP-Tb CPs + CIP (line b) and AMP-Tb CPs (line c).

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the AMP-Tb-luminol CPs with different concentrations of luminol (λex = 365 nm); (B) Fluorescent spectra of the AMP-Tb-luminol CPs with different concentrations of luminol in the presence of 2 μM CIP (λex = 365 nm).

(A) Fluorescence excitation (dashed lines) and emission (solid lines) spectra of luminol (red lines) and AMP-Tb CPs + CIP (black lines); (B) The fluorescence intensity ratio (I549/I430) of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs in the absence (blue column) and presence (red column) of CIP (2 μM) at different excitation wavelengths; (C) The effects of pH value on fluorescence intensities of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs + CIP at 430 nm (line a) and 549 nm (line b); (D) The effects of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs at 549 nm after addition of 2 μM CIP.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the AMP-Tb-luminol CPs with different concentrations of CIP (λex = 345 nm); (B) Plot of the ratios I549/I430 versus the concentration of CIP (0.005 μM, 0.01 μM, 0.05 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM, 1 μM, 1.5 μM, 2 μM, 2.5 μM).

Selectivity and competition experiments of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs towards different metal salts and biological species. (blue columns represent the luminescence I549/I430 ratios of the sensing suspension after addition of metal salts and biological species as marked, and red columns represent those in the presence of CIP). (metal salts:10 μM; biological species: 2 μM; CIP: 2 μM).
Schematic illustration of the principle of the fluorescence sensor for determination of CIP using AMP-Tb-luminol CPs.
Determination results of CIP in human blood serum.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ciprofloxacin using the terbium-based coordination polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the principle of the fluorescence sensor for determination of CIP using AMP-Tb-luminol CPs.

Herein, a facile self-assembly through adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and luminol with Tb3+ was employed to construct a dual-ligand coordinated AMP-Tb-luminol coordination polymers (CPs), which emitted the typical fluorescence of luminol. Based on the sensitization effect of ciprofloxacin (CIP) on the luminescence of Tb3+, a ratiometric sensor was fabricated using the fluorescence of luminol as an inert reference. The fluorescent intensity ratios of Tb3+ to that of luminol enhanced linearly with the CIP concentration in the range from 5 nM to 2.5 μM with a lower limit of detection of 2 nM. In addition, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibited high selectivity for CIP, which could also be used to detect CIP in human blood serum (HBS) with satisfactory results. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of using dual-ligand coordination lanthanide (Ln)-based CPs for ratio-metric CIP assay, and this straightforward strategy may open up a new platform for designing the ratio-metric sensors based on the Ln CPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a third-generation synthetic fluoroquinolone antibiotic, has been extensively used to treat bacterial infectious diseases owing to its efficient broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and lower side effects [1]. However, the excess usage and incomplete metabolization, made it easily to accumulate in body, which will cause some serious injures to human beings, such as severe liver damage and hematuria [2,3]. Therefore, it is significant to develop facile strategy to quantitatively detect CIP with high sensitivity and accuracy for human health.

Various techniques have been developed for determination of CIP, including high performance liquid chromatography, chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis, colorimetry, electrochemical method, spectrofluorimetry and so on [4–9]. Among these approaches, fluorescent method was proved to be a promising means due to its high sensitivity, quick response, low-cost, easy operation and non-destruction [10]. Especially, CIP could coordinate with lanthanide ions (Ln3+) and played as an antenna molecule to efficiently sensitize the luminescence of Ln3+ through energy transfer [11,12]. Based on the Ln3+-sensitized luminescence property, some fluorescent platforms for CIP measurement have been rationally designed and constructed [13,14], which took advantage of the excellent optical properties of Ln3+ (a wide range of fluorescent lifetimes, large Stokes and anti-Stokes shifts, and narrow band emission) [15–17]. Nevertheless, these reported fluorescent sensing systems for CIP detection mostly relied on the changes of single fluorescence intensity, which is susceptible to multiple factors including variations in probe concentration and pH value, the fluctuations originated from environment and instrument [18–21]. To solve this issue, ratiometric analysis was proposed accordingly by using the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths as the read-out signal, which can largely eliminate the interference and thus improve the stability and accuracy of the measurement [22–24]. However, there are few reports related to the Ln3+-based ratiometric sensors for CIP detection up to now.

Ln coordination polymers (Ln CPs) could be facilely obtained via the coordination reaction between Ln3+ and bridging ligands in the solution [25,26]. Apart from the easy preparation and the superior spectroscopic characteristics, they were also the ideal alternatives to fabricate the dual-emission fluorescent probes via two convenient strategies for ratio-metric assay. One is encapsulating some fluorescent material (dye molecules or quantum dots) as an internal calibration through the adaptive inclusion property [27,28]. Another is introducing an appropriate luminescent auxiliary ligand to synergistically coordinate with Ln3+ due to its large coordination numbers and high coordination flexibility [29,30]. The latter one might be more suitable to construct the stable dual-emission probes due to the covalent bonds between central metal ions and luminescent ligand, which is favor for long-duration or complicated sample measurements particularly [31–33].

In this work, we employed Tb3+ as the central metal ions, AMP and luminol as the bridging ligands, to construct the dual-ligand coordination probe AMP-Tb-luminol CPs, which emitted the fluorescence of luminol under irradiation. CIP could coordinate with Tb3+ and sensitize its emission through energy transfer. Therefore, in the presence of CIP, the fluorescence of Tb3+ emerged and efficiently enhanced with increasing the concentration of CIP, while that of luminol remained constant. This phenomenon offered a straightforward basis for the ratiometric assay for CIP.

All regents were provided commercially with analytical grade and were used as received. Adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Terbium nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3·5H2O), N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N’-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), luminol, ascorbic acid (AA), L-cysteine (Cys), glucose (Glu), L-arginine (Arg), ampicillin (AMPI), kanamycin (KANA), tetracycline (TC), and oxytetracycline (OTC) were obtained by Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) used through the study was from a Thermo Scientific Barnstead Gen Pure water purification system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). HEPES buffer (100 mM) was prepared by dissolving HEPES in ultrapure water, and the pH value was adjusted with NaOH solution (1.0 M).

A 970CRT fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai, China) with a xenon lamp as the excitation source was used to record the fluorescence spectra. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on NEXUS 470 infrared spectrometer (Nikolai Instruments Co., Ltd., USA) using KBr pellets. The morphologies of the products were characterized by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 500, Gemini, Japan).

AMP-Tb-luminol CPs were synthesized according to the self-assembly process proposed previously [31]. Briefly, 1 mL of 10 mM Tb(NO3)3 was added to 1 mL of 100 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) containing AMP (10 mM) and luminol (300 μM) under stirring. A white turbid appeared immediately and the mixture was further stirred for 3 h at room temperature. The products were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and washed three times with ultrapure water. Finally, they were re-dispersed in 1 mL HEPES buffer and stored at 4 °C prior to use.
AMP-Tb CPs were prepared through the same procedure described above without adding luminol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of CIP in aqueous solution and human blood serum (HBS)
ce:section.ce:para: For CIP assay, CIP stock solution was added to 300 μL of AMP-Tb-luminol with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 3 μM and mixed well. After a 3 min incubation, the fluorescent spectra of the detection system were measured under 345 nm excitation.

For the determination of CIP in real sample HBS, the standard addition method was employed herein. HBS was firstly 10-fold diluted using HEPES buffer (pH 7.4, 100 mM), and then was spiked with different amounts of CIP stock solution. The spiked samples were determined separately via the procedure as depicted above, and the corresponding recovery was calculated.

Same procedures were used to estimate the interference of substances, including KCl, NaCl, AA, Cys, Glu, Arg, AMPI, KANA, TC, and OTC. The final concentrations of metal salts are 10 μM, and other species are 2 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs
ce:section.ce:para: AMP can coordinate with Ln3+ in buffer solution to form the nanosized CPs through the spontaneous self-assembly process. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the obtained AMP-Tb CPs displayed adhered nanoparticles with diameters of approximate 30 nm, consistent with the previously reported nucleotide-Ln CPs [34]. The AMP-Tb CPs were non-luminescent under illumination (Fig. 2 line a), this mainly due to the small molar absorption coefficient of Tb3+, as well as the quenching effect of water molecules, which occupied the residual orbitals of Tb3+ and deactivated of the excited states via the vibration of hydroxyl [31,35,36].

To construct the ratiometric probe, a classic luminescence reagent luminol was employed as auxiliary ligands to offer an internal calibration signal. As revealed in Fig. 2 line b, after introducing luminol, the prepared CPs appeared a new broad fluorescent peak at 430 nm as compared to that of AMP-Tb CPs, which was identical to the luminescence of luminol alone. To further confirm the chemical coordination in CPs, we measured the FTIR spectra of pure AMP, luminol and AMP-Tb-luminol CPs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 , compared with AMP, the typical peaks at 1495, 1695, 970 and 1090 cm−1 belonging to the stretching vibration of N7-C8 in the adenine moiety, the P-OH stretching band, the phosphate antisymmetric and symmetric vibration bands shifted [31]. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks at 1053 and 1622 cm−1 of luminol, which were assigned to the NH2 rocking vibrations and CO stretching vibrations [30], disappeared. These illustrated that both the adenine and phosphate groups of AMP, and amino group of luminol were involved in the coordination with Tb3+. The obtained AMP-Tb-luminol CPs could also stably disperse in water, and exhibited a similar morphology to that of AMP-Tb CPs, as confirmed by SEM image in Fig. 1B. These demonstrated that both AMP and luminol coordinated with Tb3+ to form the ternary complex with little effect on the structural integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of AMP-Tb-luminol towards CIP and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of CIP, three characteristic fluorescent peaks (494, 549, and 590 nm) originated from Tb3+ emerged (Fig. 2 line c). It has been reported previously that CIP could sensitize the luminescence of Ln CPs [37]. To gain more information about the interreaction between CIP and the probe, we measured the changes in the FTIR with and without CIP. As shown in Fig. 4 , compared with AMP-Tb CPs, a new peak at 1623 cm−1 appeared after incubation with CIP (line b), which could be attributed to the COO– asymmetric stretching vibration of CIP [12,38]. In addition, the bathochromic shifts of CIP in vibration of CO, as well as the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO– were also observed. These phenomena indicated that CIP coordinated with Tb3+ in AMP-Tb CPs through the ketone group and the carboxylic acid group. Therefore, as illustrated in the Scheme 1 , the possible mechanism of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs towards CIP could be explained by the replacement of coordinating water molecules by CIP, which sensitized the emission of Tb3+ through the antenna affect, and meanwhile eliminated the quenching effect of water molecules [37,38]. Besides, the luminescence of luminol remained, serving as an inert reference for self-calibration. The enhanced fluorescence of Tb3+ was closely related to the amount of CIP, and thus the ratios of luminescence of Tb3+ to that of luminol could be used for ratio-metric sensing of CIP. Moreover, these two luminescent probes could be excited simultaneously by a single wavelength and emitted distinguishable bands, making the AMP-Tb-luminol CPs quite favorable for ratio-metric assay.

We also added different concentrations of luminol in the synthesis of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs. As expected, a gradual fluorescence increase at 430 nm was observed with increasing the amount of luminol (Fig. 5 A). However, the sensitization effect of CIP was depressed accordingly (Fig. 5B), verifying a lower response to be achieved with more luminol molecules involved in the CPs. Thus, the appropriate concentration of luminol was set to be of 300 μM to prepare the AMP-Tb-luminol CPs.

To achieve a better analytical performance of the proposed assay for CIP, some factors seriously affected the measurement were optimized. As shown in Fig. 6 A, luminol and Tb3+ sensitized by CIP could be excited at 345–370 nm, and displayed clearly distinguishable emission bands. This rendered it suited for the single-wavelength excitation operation, which was more convenient than the system needing two excitation wavelengths to excite the two luminescent constituents in the ratiometric assay. Upon excitation at 345 nm, the highest fluorescent intensity ratio I549/I430 as well as the biggest difference of ratios in the absence and presence of 2 μM CIP were achieved (Fig. 6B). Thus, 345 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for increasing the higher read-out signal and sensitivity for CIP measurement.

During our experiment we noticed that the pH value of HEPES buffer strongly affected the fluorescence intensities of the two illuminants, and thus the ratios. As shown in Fig. 6C, the luminescence of luminol decreased with increasing the pH value from 6.0 to 10.0. In the presence of CIP, the fluorescence of Tb3+ at 549 nm revealed a small decrease when the pH value changed from 6.0 to 7.4, and then a spike of fluorescence decrease with increasing pH value, suggesting weakly acidic and neutral media were more beneficial for CIP determination. Therefore, considering the pH-dependent fluorescence properties of the dual-emission probe and the convenient analysis of biological sample, pH 7.4 was set for the following experiments.

We then investigated the time-dependent fluorescence changes of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs upon addition of CIP. When CIP was added, an obvious fluorescence enhancement at 549 nm was observed in the initial 1 min, and then a plateau reached at about 3 min (Fig. 6D). Because the luminescence of luminol almost remained constant in this course, the time-dependent ratio changes were identical to that of the fluorescence changes at 549 nm. To achieve the high sensitivity, after the addition of CIP, the detection system was incubated 3 min and then the fluorescent spectra were recorded for the analysis.

Under the optimized conditions, a ratiometric CIP assay was investigated. As seen in Fig. 7 A, the three typical green emission of Tb3+ centred at 494, 549, and 590 nm increased gradually with the increase of concentration of CIP from 0 to 2.5 μM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of luminol with peak at 430 nm remained constant, serving as an inert reference. By plotting the fluorescence intensity ratios of I549/I430 versus the concentration of CIP, a good linearity was obtained in the concentration range from 5 nM to 2.5 μM with a correlation of 0.9987 (Fig. 7B). The resulting linear equation could be expressed as I549/I430 = 0.5877 + 0.9451CCIP, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2 nM (3σ). The LOD was comparable or much lower than those of previously reported ratio-metric methods for CIP assay by using Si nanoparticles or carbon dots [39,40].

Next, we evaluated the selectivity of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs for CIP by measuring the response of some potential interferences, such as metal salts (KCl, NaCl), amino acids (Cys, Arg), small molecules (AA, Glu), and other antibiotics (AMPI, KANA, TC, OTC). As shown in Fig. 8 (blue column), only CIP could induce remarkable changes in the fluorescence intensity ratios I549/I430. Other substances almost had no effects on the read-out signal. Besides, other coexisting substances involved had no obvious effects on the detection of CIP (Fig. 8 red column). These results showed that the proposed method had high selectivity and anti-interference ability for CIP.

To further estimate the practical application of AMP-Tb-luminol CPs, we measured the levels of CIP in HBS samples. A standard addition method was employed and the results were listed in Table 1 . For the four spiked HBS samples, the recoveries of CIP were between 96.4 and 101.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) were all less than 1.6%. This good recovery and precision enabled the present method used for determination of CIP in the HBS sample.

In summary, we have presented a facile strategy to prepare the dual-ligand coordinated AMP-Tb-luminol CPs, and further proved its practical application in ratio-metric determination of CIP in aqueous solution and real sample HBS with high sensitivity and selectivity. The proposed sensor took advantage of the easy synthesis of probes, and the superior properties of Ln CPs, as well as the high accuracy of the ratio-metric type, making it very facile, simple and more accurate for CIP detection. Given the diversity and the versatility of the target-stimulated response, Ln CPs may be readily adapted to develop a wide variety of ratio-metric sensors.

Xinan An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xinyue Zhu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jiaojiao Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Lina Zou: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation. Gaiping Li: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Project administration. Baoxian Ye: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21575130).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Topoisomerase IV-quinolone interactions are mediated through a water-metal ion bridge: mechanistic basis of quinolone resistance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Aldred, S.A. McPherson, C.L. Turnbough, Jr., R.J. Kerns, N. Osheroff, Topoisomerase IV-quinolone interactions are mediated through a water-metal ion bridge: mechanistic basis of quinolone resistance, Nucleic acids research, 41(8) (2013) 4628-4639.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ciprofloxacin-induced acute liver injury: case report and review of literature

F. Garlando, M.G. Tauber, B. Joos, O. Oelz, R. Luthy, Ciprofloxacin-induced hematuria, Infection, 13(4) (1985) 177-178.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance liquid chromatographic determination of ciprofloxacin in plasma samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Vybiralova, M. Nobilis, J. Zoulova, J. Kvetina, P. Petr, High-performance liquid chromatographic determination of ciprofloxacin in plasma samples, Journal of pharmaceutical and biomedical analysis, 37(5) (2005) 851-858.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence determination of fluoroquinolones using Fenton system in the presence of terbium(iii) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kaczmarek, S. Lis, Chemiluminescence determination of fluoroquinolones using Fenton system in the presence of terbium(iii) ions, The Analyst, 136(12) (2011) 2592-2597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of quinolone antibiotics in urine by capillary electrophoresis with chemiluminescence detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Liu, Y.X. Jia, W. Tian, Determination of quinolone antibiotics in urine by capillary electrophoresis with chemiluminescence detection, Journal of separation science, 31(21) (2008) 3765-3771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective electrochemical detection of ciprofloxacin with a porous nafion/multiwalled carbon nanotube composite film electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Gayen, B.P. Chaplin, Selective Electrochemical Detection of Ciprofloxacin with a Porous Nafion/Multiwalled Carbon Nanotube Composite Film Electrode, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 8(3) (2016) 1615-1626.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrofluorometric determination of certain quinolone antibacterials using metal chelation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. El-Kommos, Spectrofluorometric determination of certain quinolone antibacterials using metal chelation, Talanta, 60(5) (2003) 1033-1050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric aptamer based assay for the determination of fluoroquinolones by triggering the reduction-catalyzing activity of gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Lavaee, N.M. Danesh, M. Ramezani, K. Abnous, S.M. Taghdisi, Colorimetric aptamer based assay for the determination of fluoroquinolones by triggering the reduction-catalyzing activity of gold nanoparticles, Microchimica Acta, 184(7) (2017) 2039-2045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activatable formation of emissive excimers for highly selective detection of beta-galactosidase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, L. Ning, F. Yuan, T. Zhang, J. Zhang, Z. Xu, X.F. Yang, Activatable Formation of Emissive Excimers for Highly Selective Detection of beta-Galactosidase, Analytical chemistry, 92(8) (2020) 5733-5740.
M.D. Allendorf, C.A. Bauer, R.K. Bhakta, R.J. Houk, Luminescent metal-organic frameworks, Chemical Society reviews, 38(5) (2009) 1330-1352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Terbium-based coordination polymer nanoparticles for detection of ciprofloxacin in tablets and biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tan, L. Zhang, C. Ma, Y. Song, F. Xu, S. Chen, L. Wang, Terbium-based coordination polymer nanoparticles for detection of ciprofloxacin in tablets and biological fluids, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 5(22) (2013) 11791-11796.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of ciprofloxacin and enrofloxacin by the sensitized fluorescence of europium in the presence of the second ligand and micelles of anionic surfactants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Shtykov, T.D. Smirnova, Y.G. Bylinkin, N.V. Kalashnikova, D.A. Zhemerichkin, Determination of ciprofloxacin and enrofloxacin by the sensitized fluorescence of europium in the presence of the second ligand and micelles of anionic surfactants, Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 62(2) (2007) 136-140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of ciprofloxacin in urine through sensitized lanthanide luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Singha, K.H. Ahn, Detection of Ciprofloxacin in Urine through Sensitized Lanthanide Luminescence, Sensors, 16(12) (2016).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks for luminescent sensing and light-emitting applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Cui, B. Chen, G. Qian, Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks for luminescent sensing and light-emitting applications, Coordination Chemistry Reviews, 273-274 (2014) 76-86.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in lanthanide-based luminescent probes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide luminescence for functional materials and bio-sciences

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.V. Eliseeva, J.C. Bunzli, Lanthanide luminescence for functional materials and bio-sciences, Chemical Society reviews, 39(1) (2010) 189-227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Protein-templated gold nanoclusters based sensor for off-on detection of ciprofloxacin with a high selectivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Chen, S. Qian, J. Chen, J. Cai, S. Wu, Z. Cai, Protein-templated gold nanoclusters based sensor for off-on detection of ciprofloxacin with a high selectivity, Talanta, 94 (2012) 240-245.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescence probe Eu3+ functionalized Ga-MOF integrated with logic gate operation for detecting ppm-level ciprofloxacin (CIP) in urine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, B. Yan, A turn-on fluorescence probe Eu3+ functionalized Ga-MOF integrated with logic gate operation for detecting ppm-level ciprofloxacin (CIP) in urine, Talanta, 208 (2020) 120438.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bipyridine- and copper-functionalized N-doped carbon dots for fluorescence turn off-on detection of ciprofloxacin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.D. Dang, A.B. Ganganboina, R.A. Doong, Bipyridine- and Copper-Functionalized N-doped Carbon Dots for Fluorescence Turn Off-On Detection of Ciprofloxacin, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 12(29) (2020) 32247-32258.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly stable, rapid and sensitive fluorescent probe for ciprofloxacin based on Al3+-enhanced fluorescence of gold nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Fu, X. Zheng, H. Li, L. Ding, F. Wang, D.Y. Guo, W. Yang, Q. Pan, A highly stable, rapid and sensitive fluorescent probe for ciprofloxacin based on Al3+-enhanced fluorescence of gold nanoclusters, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 346 (2021) 130502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles: towards ratiometric fluorescent sensing of cerebrospinal Abeta monomer as a biomarker for Alzheimer's disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, D. Lu, X. You, G. Shi, J. Deng, T. Zhou, Carbon dots sensitized lanthanide infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles: Towards ratiometric fluorescent sensing of cerebrospinal Abeta monomer as a biomarker for Alzheimer's disease, Analytica chimica acta, 1105 (2020) 147-154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence method for malachite green detection based on dual-emission BSA-protected gold nanoclusters
